draw.question: In what order should you work through the sections of the guide, and which sections cover what matters most?
draw.expectedAnswer: Work through the sections in order; the earlier sections cover the preparations that matter most.

draw.question: If a storm is forecast and you have advance warning, what should you also fill, and what is that water for?
draw.expectedAnswer: also fill the bathtub; bathtub water is for flushing toilets and washing, never for drinking.

draw.question: What are the good non-perishable food choices the guide lists for the emergency shelf?
draw.expectedAnswer: canned beans, canned fish, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and shelf-stable milk.

draw.question: For about how long does a full freezer hold a safe temperature, and for about how long does a half-full freezer hold it?
draw.expectedAnswer: A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about forty-eight hours, but a half-full freezer holds it for only about twenty-four hours.

draw.question: How many battery-powered carbon monoxide alarms should you install, and where?
draw.expectedAnswer: Install at least one battery-powered carbon monoxide alarm on each floor where people sleep.

draw.question: Below what indoor temperature, if you cannot maintain warmth, should you relocate, and where should you go?
draw.expectedAnswer: If the indoor temperature falls below ten degrees Celsius and you cannot maintain warmth, relocate to a warming centre or a neighbour's home.

draw.question: For up to how long does refrigerated medication such as insulin generally remain usable at room temperature, and who should confirm the rule for your specific medication?
draw.expectedAnswer: Refrigerated medication such as insulin generally remains usable at room temperature for up to twenty-eight days, but confirm the rule for your specific medication with a pharmacist before an emergency happens.

draw.question: Whose phone numbers should you write down on paper, and why?
draw.expectedAnswer: Write down the phone numbers of your pharmacy, your doctor, and the poison-control line on paper, because a dead phone is not a phone book.

step1_numbered_facts.method: Read artifact/GUIDE.md (the ORIGINAL guide). Split the body into sentences on '.', '!', '?' boundaries in reading order; indexed every sentence stating a checkable fact (quantity, duration, temperature, item, instruction) from F1 upward. 32 facts total, F1..F32.

facts.F1: This guide explains how to prepare an ordinary household to manage safely and comfortably through a power outage lasting up to seventy-two hours.
facts.F2: It is written for a temperate climate and assumes no backup generator.
facts.F3: Work through the sections in order; the earlier sections cover the preparations that matter most.
facts.F4: Store four litres of water per person per day, which means twelve litres per person for the full seventy-two hours.
facts.F5: Half of that allowance is for drinking, and the other half covers cooking and basic hygiene.
facts.F6: Store the water in food-grade containers, keep the containers out of direct sunlight, and replace stored water every six months.
facts.F7: If a storm is forecast and you have advance warning, also fill the bathtub; bathtub water is for flushing toilets and washing, never for drinking.
facts.F8: Keep a dedicated shelf of non-perishable food sufficient for three days.
facts.F9: Good choices are canned beans, canned fish, peanut butter, crackers, dried fruit, and shelf-stable milk.
facts.F10: Choose foods that require no cooking, and make sure you own a manual can opener, because an electric one will be useless.
facts.F11: Eat the perishable food from the refrigerator first, then the food from the freezer, and only then open the emergency shelf.
facts.F12: An unopened refrigerator keeps food safe for about four hours.
facts.F13: A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about forty-eight hours, but a half-full freezer holds it for only about twenty-four hours, so it is worth keeping the freezer consolidated and full, even if some of the space is taken up by containers of frozen water.
facts.F14: Keep both doors closed as much as possible and tape a note to each door as a reminder.
facts.F15: Any perishable food that has spent more than two hours above four degrees Celsius should be discarded; when in doubt, throw it out.
facts.F16: Use battery-powered LED lanterns or headlamps as your primary light source, and avoid candles entirely, since candles are a leading cause of house fires during outages.
facts.F17: Store at least one light per person, plus two spare sets of batteries for each light.
facts.F18: Keep one headlamp beside each bed and one lantern in the kitchen, and place them where they can be found in the dark.
facts.F19: Never run a camping stove, barbecue, or generator indoors, in a garage, or near a window, because each of these produces carbon monoxide, which is odourless and can be fatal.
facts.F20: Install at least one battery-powered carbon monoxide alarm on each floor where people sleep.
facts.F21: In cold weather, choose one small room on a south-facing side of the home, close its door, and gather everyone there; layered clothing and blankets are safer than any improvised heater.
facts.F22: If the indoor temperature falls below ten degrees Celsius and you cannot maintain warmth, relocate to a warming centre or a neighbour's home.
facts.F23: Keep a battery-powered or hand-crank radio for emergency broadcasts, and know the frequency of your local emergency station before the outage begins.
facts.F24: Charge every phone and power bank whenever a storm is forecast; a full modern power bank of twenty thousand milliamp-hours will recharge a typical phone roughly four times.
facts.F25: Put your phone in low-power mode at the start of the outage, and agree in advance on one out-of-area contact whom every member of the household will text, because text messages get through congested networks more reliably than voice calls.
facts.F26: Keep a first-aid kit and a seven-day supply of every prescription medication, and rotate the medications so they stay in date.
facts.F27: Refrigerated medication such as insulin generally remains usable at room temperature for up to twenty-eight days, but confirm the rule for your specific medication with a pharmacist before an emergency happens.
facts.F28: Write down the phone numbers of your pharmacy, your doctor, and the poison-control line on paper, because a dead phone is not a phone book.
facts.F29: Wait five minutes before switching major appliances back on, so the grid can stabilise.
facts.F30: Check the freezer: if food still contains ice crystals, it can be safely refrozen.
facts.F31: Restock everything used during the outage within one week, starting with water and batteries.
facts.F32: Finally, write a one-page note about what was missing or awkward, and fix at least one of those gaps before the next outage arrives.

step2_serialization_and_hash.serialization_rule: Canonical JSON: parse the verbatim proposal artifact, re-emit with recursively sorted object keys, no whitespace, array order preserved. Written as the exact bytes of proposal_canon.json (5857 bytes).
step2_serialization_and_hash.canonicalizer: runs/r3/r3.1/p1-compose-line-edit-over-best/canon.mjs (JSON.parse then recursive canon() that sorts Object.keys and joins with no whitespace); input runs/.../raw_proposal.json is the verbatim artifact.
step2_serialization_and_hash.verify: sha256sum of the saved proposal_canon.json equals seedHex; re-derivable by any third party from proposal_canon.json alone.

step3_draw.rule: Digest bytes read left to right as unsigned integers b0,b1,... (b0=0xa8=168, b1=0x25=37, b2=0x46=70, b3=0xe3=227, b4=0x8e=142, b5=0xb3=179, b6=0x49=73, b7=0x73=115). Remaining fact list starts as [F1..F32] in order. For draw k: idx = b_k mod (remaining count); pick and remove element at that 0-based index; draw without replacement.

transcript.notes: Seat HOLD-APART (the Gap). Witnesses derived Fiat-Shamir from the proposal artifact alone; no proposer-suggested witnesses accepted (GR-4). proposal_canon.json left in place for the auditor.
